coredata.dc:title: A novel “OFF-ON” biosensor based on nanosurface energy transfer between gold nanocrosses and graphene quantum dots for intracellular ATP sensing and tracking
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruyuan
Sun, Jiadi
Ji, Jian
Pi, Fuwei
Xiao, Yan
Zhang, Yinzhi
Sun, Xiulan
coredata.dc:description: An efficient strategy based on the nanosurface energy transfer (NSET) between gold nanocrosses (AuNCs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for intracellular ATP sensing and tracking was first developed. GQDs and AuNCs assembled in the bases of complementary matching principle, which result in the fluorescence quenching via NSET. In the presence of ATP, aptamer recognized and combined with ATP, caused the distance change between GQDs and AuNCs. The fluorescence intensity was proportional to ATP concentration, which was represented by the equation y = 176.67 x + 335.51 (R2 = 0.9916) with the detection limit of 0.27 mM (3δ, n = 11). Moreover, the proposed “OFF-ON” sensor showed low cytotoxicity and excellent performance in intracellular ATP sensing, which will provide new possibilities for labeling of small biological molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: gold nanocrosses
graphene quantum dots
ATP
sensing
tracking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assemble of GQDs-AuNCs and detecting of ATP
Analytical performance of the AuNCs-GQDs sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the AuNCs-GQDs assembly and intracellular ATP sensing process.

Characterization of the FA-GQDs-ABA, AuNCs-ssDNA, and AuNCs-GQDs. TEM images of AuNCs (A), GQDs (B), and AuNCs-GQDs (C). HR-TEM images of AuNCs-GQDs (D), (E). (F) Zeta potential of the nanoparticles during modification (a) GQDs -8.23 mV, (b) FA-GQDs-ABA -25.28 mV, (c) AuNCs 51.06 mV, (d) AuNCs-PEG 3.03 mV, and (e) AuNCs-ssDNA -14.28 mV.
Spectra analysis of the assembly formation. UV-vis absorption of the FA-GQDs-ABA (A), AuNCs-ssDNA (B), and AuNCs-GQDs (C) formation. (D) FTIR spectra of the GQDs-ABA, GQDs-FA, and FA-GQDs-ABA.
Fluorescence intensity at different ATP concentrations (0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.6, 1.8, 2.0, and 4 mM). (B) The standard curve of ATP detection.

Specificity of the sensor toward UTP, CTP, GTP, and ATP at a concentration of 1.8 mM. △F meant the fluorescence intensity after blank deduction. Quantitative data were presented as mean ± SD with n=3.

Flow-cytometric analysis of apoptosis in (a) MCF-7 cells and (b) MCF-7 cells treated with AuNCs-GQDs. (c) HepG2 cells and (d) HepG2 cells treated with AuNCs-GQDs. (e) HeLa cells and (f) HeLa cells treated with AuNCs-GQDs.

(A) Confocal images of HeLa, HepG2, and MCF-7 cells incubated with AuNCs-GQDs for 2 h. Scale bar: 20 μm. (B) Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with AuNCs-GQDs assembled by random DNA and ABA. Scale bar: 20 μm.
Comparision of linear range and LOD of different methods for ATP detection.

head.ce:title: A novel “OFF-ON” biosensor based on nanosurface energy transfer between gold nanocrosses and graphene quantum dots for intracellular ATP sensing and tracking

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science, State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center for Functional Foods, School of Food Science Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and Nutrition, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center for Functional Foods
School of Food Science Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and Nutrition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou GRG Metrology & Test Co., Ltd, Guangzhou, 510000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou GRG Metrology & Test Co., Ltd

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient strategy based on the nanosurface energy transfer (NSET) between gold nanocrosses (AuNCs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for intracellular ATP sensing and tracking was first developed. GQDs and AuNCs assembled in the bases of complementary matching principle, which result in the fluorescence quenching via NSET. In the presence of ATP, aptamer recognized and combined with ATP, caused the distance change between GQDs and AuNCs. The fluorescence intensity was proportional to ATP concentration, which was represented by the equation y = 176.67 x + 335.51 (R2 = 0.9916) with the detection limit of 0.27 mM (3δ, n = 11). Moreover, the proposed “OFF-ON” sensor showed low cytotoxicity and excellent performance in intracellular ATP sensing, which will provide new possibilities for labeling of small biological molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: The dynamics and distributions of intracellular biomolecules are important for understanding physiological processes, diagnosing diseases, and determining the target location for treatments. Compared with normal cells, cancer cells exhibit different concentrations of many small biological molecules, such as sulfhydryl compounds, microRNAs, and adenosine-5’-triphosphate (ATP). As an example, the main role of ATP in cells is to provide energy and participate in the metabolism of fats, proteins, sugars, and nucleic acids. The measurement of intracellular ATP concentration can reflect both the state of the cells as well as provide insights into the effects of different external factors on the cells. Marcel Leist et al. found that ATP concentration was a switch in the decision between apoptosis and necrosis [1]. It has been reported that intracellular ATP levels were a pivotal determinant of chemoresistance in colon cancer cells [2]. Zhanxia Zhang et al. [3] found that the ATP content in cancer cells was much higher than normal cells due to their strengthened metabolic activity. However, traditional ATP detection methods, such as chemiluminescence and bioluminescence based on fluorescein [4], require extraction of intracellular ATP; thus, the procedures are trival, the reproducibility is poor and the process is time-consuming. As such, the development of new strategies for direct and non-invasive ATP imaging in living cells is of great importance.

Compared with other imaging techniques, such as ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computed tomography (CT), fluorescence imaging technology has the characteristics of high spatial resolution, safe operation, low cost, and intuitive and reliable results. The advancement of novel fluorescent materials further makes fluorescence imaging an upsurge in scientific research [5,6]. Compared with traditional organic dyes, GQDs have many unique properties, such as excellent electro-optical properties, unique fluorescence stability, and low cytotoxicity [7]. In addition, they are rich in sources [8] and easy to be chemically modified [9,10]. However, simple cell imaging only based on GQDs does not meet the requirements for small molecule sensing, and the always “ON” fluorescence state results in a high rate of false positives [11]. As such, the design of molecular “OFF-ON” switches can widen the application of GQDs in the areas of small intracellular biomolecules sensing.

Noble metal nanomaterials have special physical and chemical properties and have been widely used in catalysis [12], biological labelling [13], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [14], and other fields [15]. One performance of metallic nanomaterials that should be emphasized is nanosurface energy transfer (NSET), which refers to the energy transfer from a molecular dipole to a metal nanosurface [16], has longer effect distance than Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The NSET between noble metal nanomaterials and GQDs leads to the attenuation of the fluorescence signal (described as an “OFF” state), which can be exploited to design excellent strategy for small biomolecules sensing in cells. Compared with “ON-OFF’’ sensors, “OFF-ON” sensors have a higher signal-to-noise ratio and less nonspecific adsorption [17–19]. Hua Kuang et al. [20] developed an assembly of spherical gold nanoparticles and quantum dots (QDs), which functioned as an ultrasensitive “OFF-ON” switch for intracellular microRNA detection. Wei Wei et al. [21] developed an effective sensing platform based on FRET from QDs to graphene oxide (GO). Compared with spherical gold nanoparticles, gold nanorods (AuNRs) and their subtle variant gold nanocrosses (AuNCs) possess strong plasmonic fields, large specific surface areas, excellent tenability and exceptional biocompatibility, have been regarded as a promising new nanostructure in the areas of biomedical and biological research. However, as far as we know, there has been no report regarding the assembling of AuNCs and GQDs for target sensing. Inspired by their enhanced electromagnetic field at the tips, we developed an “OFF-ON” sensor for intracellular ATP sensing. Moreover, in our strategy the ATP was integrated with GQDs at an “ON” state, which allowed the tracking of the ATP molecule via the fluorescence signal.

DNA-assembled nanomaterials exhibit controllable morphologies and properties [22]. Meanwhile, DNA is protected from enzyme degradation due to steric hindrance of nanomaterials. Here, based on Watson-Crick base pairing, we chose AuNCs and GQDs for the construction of a novel NSET system. Furthermore, folic acid (FA) was modified on the GQDs. FA is a high-affinity ligand of the folic acid receptor (FR) [23], which has high expression levels in several tumors and is present at extremely low levels in normal tissues [24]. Thus, the FA modified AuNCs-GQDs could enhance endocytosis via the FR into FR + cancer cells. In this regard, the proposed “OFF-ON” sensor would serve as an excellent strategy for ATP sensing and tracking in FR + cancer cells.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4; 99%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99%), sodium salicylate (98%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99%), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), L-ascorbic acid (99.5%), thiolated polyethylene glycol-5000 (SH-PEG5000, 95%), tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP, 98%), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), folic acid (FA, 97%) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Commercial graphene quantum dots (GQDs, 1 mg/mL) was purchased from XFNANO (Nanjing, China). All oligonucleotides were synthesised from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). Sequences were listed in table S1. ATP (95%), UTP (95%), GTP (95%), and CTP (95%) were from Yuanye Bio-Technology (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (Millipore, Ultrapure Water Purification Systems) was employed to prepare solutions. Fetal bovine serum and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) were purchased from Gibco Invitrogen Corporation (CA, USA); HepG2 cells, MCF-7 cells, and HeLa cells were obtained from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China).

UV–vis spectra were performed on an Arantes Avaspec-2048UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (NICOLET MEXUS 470, Thermo Electron Corporation) was used to obtain infrared spectra. Transmission electron microscopy images were acquired using a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR). The zeta potentials were made with Malvern Zetasizer nano ZS. Fluorescence spectra were obtained from a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi); Conditions of fluorescence spectra were as follows: excitation wavelength, 395 nm; emission wavelength, 400 ∼ 500 nm. HeLa, HepG2, and MCF-7 cells were incubated in a CO2 incubator (Thermo Scientific Forma Series II Water Jacket, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Rockford, IL, USA). Flow cytometry tests were acquired using a FACSCalibur Flow cytometry. Fluorescence microscopy images were performed on LSM710 Laser confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss AG, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assemble of GQDs-AuNCs and detecting of ATP
ce:section.ce:para: The AuNCs were synthesized using an improved seed-mediated growth method [25]. PEG and DNA modified AuNCs were prepared as the methods we reported before [26]. FA-GQDs-ABA were prepared using the method of Xiaoqian Su described in a previous report [27]. AuNCs-ssDNA (0.5 mL) and FA-GQDs-ABA (0.5 mL) were incubated in the constant temperature oscillator (37℃) for 12 h. Then, the obtained conjugates were washed with water by centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The “OFF-ON” biosensor for ATP sensing was completed. Different concentrations of ATP were added to the GQDs-AuNCs system and incubation for 2 h to ensure complete reaction between ABA and ATP.

MCF-7, HepG2, and HeLa cells were used in this study as model cells, all of which for experiments were at the logarithmic phase of growth. Cells were cultured in DMEM containing 10% FBS at 37℃ in a 5% CO2 incubator. Flow-cytometric analysis was used for evaluating apoptosis. Briefly, MCF-7, HepG2, and HeLa cells were cultured in 6-well plates with a density of 2.0 × 105 cells/well for 12 h. Then, the cell medium was removed and replaced by 2 mL fresh DMEM containing 5.8 pM AuNCs-GQDs. After incubation for 12 h, the cells were harvested, washed, and stained with AnnexinV-FITC and propidium iodide following the instructions. Finally, flow cytometer was used for cells analysis.

Cell imaging was used for detecting intracellular ATP. Briefly, MCF-7, HepG2, and HeLa cells were cultured on glass-bottomed Petri dishes with a density of 2.0 × 104 cells/dish for 12 h. After the attachment of cells, cell medium was removed and replaced by 0.5 mL fresh DMEM containing 5.8 pM AuNCs-GQDs each dish. After incubation for 2 h, cells were washed with PBS to remove the extracellular AuNCs-GQDs. Finally, Laser confocal microscope was used for cell imaging with the excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , carboxylic GQDs were covalently conjugated with the ATP-binding aptamer (ABA) and FA via an amine-carboxyl linking in the presence of EDC and NHS. ABA can specifically recognize ATP, while the FA can differentiate FR + and FR- cells; thus, FA-GQDs-ABA should be able to distinguish the ATP presented in FR + cancer cells. Single strand DNA (ssDNA), which was partly complementary to ABA, could link to AuNCs through S-Au bond. After incubation at 37 °C, GQDs and AuNCs assembled via complementary base pairing. The GQDs could emit stable blue fluorescence under an excitation wavelength of 395 nm, which was initially quenched by the AuNCs via NSET. In the presence of ATP, the NSET between GQDs and AuNCs vanished, as ABA specifically recognized ATP and GQDs detached from AuNCs, resulting in the restoration of the fluorescence signal.

The AuNCs were demonstrated good morphology under TEM (Fig. 1 A). As shown in Fig. 1 B, GQDs had good water dispersity and a uniform size, with an average diameter of approximately 7 nm. Fig. 1 C showed the TEM image of AuNCs-ssDNA hybridized with complementary FA-GQDs-ABA, demonstrating the successful assembly of GQDs-AuNCs. The HR-TEM (Fig. 1 D, E) indicated that the GQDs situated around the AuNCs had a lattice fringe of about 0.21 nm and that the distance between the GQDs and AuNCs was about 4 nm, which was in accord with the length of the 13-base complementary region between ABA and ssDNA. Fig. 1 F showed the zeta potential of the nanoassembly formation. After conjugation with ABA and FA, the zeta potential of the GQDs decreased from -8.23 mV to -25.28 mV, implying the successful covalent attachment of ABA and FA to the GQDs. After the incubation of AuNCs and PEG, the zeta potential of AuNCs changed from 51.06 mV to 3.03 mV, indicating that most of the CTAB on the surface of the AuNCs was replaced by SH-PEG. After ssDNA linked to AuNCs-PEG, the zeta potential of AuNCs changed from positive to negative, demonstrating the successful assembly of AuNCs-ssDNA via the Au-S bond. The hydrodynamic diameter of the nanoassembly formation was shown in Fig. S2 A. After modification, the hydrodynamic diameter of the AuNCs was obviously increased. As can be seen, the larger size of the AuNCs-GQDs indicated the successful assembly.

To further characterize the formation of the AuNCs-GQDs, the UV-vis spectra of FA-GQDs-ABA (Fig. 2 A), AuNCs-ssDNA (Fig. 2B), and AuNCs-GQDs (Fig. 2C) were assessed. The absorption spectrum of FA-GQDs-ABA showed the characteristic peaks of FA, GQDs, and ABA at 280 nm, 330 nm, and 260 nm, respectively, demonstrating the successful formation of FA-GQDs-ABA. The longitudinal surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peak of AuNCs was 650 nm. After modification with PEG, the LSPR band at 650 nm blue shifted to 628 nm. The absorption spectrum of AuNCs-ssDNA continued to blue shift to 590 nm and showed an absorption peak of ssDNA at around 260 nm. The reason why it blue shifted was because the overall dielectric constant of the medium between AuNCs and GQDs changed after modification with PEG and ssDNA, which was in agreement with the results of previous study [28,29]. The UV-vis spectra of AuNCs-ssDNA and AuNCs-GQDs were similar, confirming that there was no AuNCs aggregation after the assembly of AuNCs and GQDs. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum of GQDs-ssDNA (Fig. 2D) showed peaks of C = O stretch and N-H bending, which indicated the formation of an amide bond between GQDs and ABA. Similarly, FA can covalently bind to GQDs in the same way. Hence, FA-GQDs-ABA showed the characteristic peaks attributed to the amide bonds of GQDs-ABA and GQDs-FA. DNA polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) (Fig. S2B) further verified the successful combination of FA-GQDs-ABA and AuNCs-ssDNA.

ATP is an important molecule that functions as a universal energy currency in living species. It serves to regulate cellular metabolism and biochemical pathways, and it is also an indicator of cell injury and cell viability. Generally, ATP is concentrated in the intracellular cytosol [30–32]. In this study, ATP was chosen as a model molecule for our proof-of-concept experiments. GQDs had an emission peak of 456 nm under UV excitation at 395 nm. To test the feasibility of the proposed bio-system, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was detected after addition of AuNCs and ATP. As shown in Fig. S3 A, fluorescence intensity decreased with the increasing proportion of AuNCs, which was attributed to the larger number of free GQDs absorbed on the surface of AuNCs, thus exhibited the higher quenching efficiency. As indicated in Fig. S3B, after incubation with AuNCs at 37 °C for 12 h, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreased, while in the presence of 1.8 mM ATP, there was a significantly enhanced fluorescent signal. These results demonstrated that the AuNCs-GQDs system could effectively respond to the ATP due to the specific recognition conferred by ABA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the AuNCs-GQDs sensor

ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the AuNCs-GQDs assembly was assessed in HeLa, HepG2, and MCF-7 cells using a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay and flow-cytometric analysis. As shown in Fig. S4A, when the concentration of AuNCs-GQDs was below 5.8 pM, the cell viability was greater than 90% and there was no obvious cytotoxicity. Flow-cytometric analysis results (Fig. 5 ) further demonstrated that there was no large difference in apoptosis rates between the cells with/without AuNCs-GQDs treatment at the concentration of 5.8 pM. Moreover, the AuNCs-GQDs exhibited low hemolytic activity (Fig. S5).

Laser confocal imaging was performed to study the intracellular sensing performance of the bio-system. Time-dependent analytical performances of AuNCs-GQDs in HeLa cells were shown in Fig. S6. Three kinds of cancer cells, HeLa (FR+), HepG2 (FR+), and MCF-7 (FR-) were incubated with AuNCs-GQDs for 2 h at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 6 A, HeLa and HepG2 cells exhibited bright blue fluorescence compared with MCF-7 cells, confirming that the biosensor could specifically recognize FR + cells. As shown in Fig. 6B, when the ABA was replaced with a random DNA under the same experimental conditions, there was no obvious fluorescence signal. This indicated that it was ATP that resulted in the fluorescence recovery of the GQDs. Moreover, after treatment with oligomycin, which is known to reduce intracellular ATP concentration, HeLa cells demonstrated a lower fluorescence signal (Fig. S4B).

In summary, we have developed a new biosensor based on the NSET between AuNCs and GQDs for intracellular ATP sensing and tracking. ABA modified FA-GQDs and ssDNA modified AuNCs assembled via complementary base pairing. To our knowledge, there is no prior report on the assembly of AuNCs and FA-GQDs that combined the advantages of the enhanced electromagnetic field at the tips of AuNCs, unique fluorescence stability and low cytotoxicity of GQDs and the high-affinity of FA towards FR + cancer cells. The proposed “OFF-ON” sensor exhibited excellent sensitivity and specificity, with a response range closer to actual intracellular ATP levels, making this sensor more conducive to intracellular ATP sensing. The cell experiments showed that the AuNCs-GQDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity and possessed outstanding properties in intracellular ATP sensing and tracking. Moreover, the proposed strategy should function universally following changes to the DNA sequence to that of another target; here we chose ATP as a model molecule for proof of concept. We believe that this GQDs-AuNCs sensor has great potential for targeting small molecules in FR + cancer cells and could be a versatile technology for application in biomedical imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFC1601700, 2016YFD0401204), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (No. BE2016691)， Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160178), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603087), National first-class discipline program of Food Science and Technology (JUFSTR20180303)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.141.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Aptamer-based Colorimetric Biosensing of Ochratoxin A in Fortified White Grape Wine Sample Using Unmodified Gold Nanoparticles
Energy Transfer Sensitization of Luminescent Gold Nanoclusters: More than Just the Classical Förster Mechanism
Electrochemiluminescent detection of mucin 1 protein and MCF-7 cancer cells based on the resonance energy transfer
A convenient and label-free fluorescence “turn off–on” nanosensor with high sensitivity and selectivity for acid phosphatase
A fluorescence method for detection of DNA and DNA methylation based on graphene oxide and restriction endonuclease HpaII
Gold-Quantum Dot Core-Satellite Assemblies for Lighting Up MicroRNA In Vitro and In Vivo
Detection of MUC-1 protein and MCF-7 cells based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer from quantum dots to graphene oxide
Light-Harvesting Nanoparticle Core-Shell Clusters with Controllable Optical Output
Structural basis for molecular recognition of folic acid by folate receptors
Folate-mediated delivery of macromolecular anticancer therapeutic agents
DNA biosensor-based on fluorescence detection of E. coli O157:H7 by Au@Ag nanorods
An Ultrasensitive "FRET-SEF" Probe for Sensing and Imaging MicroRNAs in Living Cells Based on Gold Nano-Conjugates
A graphene quantum dot@Fe3O4@SiO2 based nanoprobe for drug delivery sensing and dual-modal fluorescence and MRI imaging in cancer cells
Site-Specific Surface Functionalization of Gold Nanorods using DNA Origami Clamps
Journal of the American Chemical Society
Blood clearance and tissue distribution of PEGylated and non-PEGylated gold nanorods after intravenous administration in rats
A novel method for measurement of submembrane ATP concentration

An ultrasensitive electrochemical DNA sensor based on the ssDNA-assisted cascade of hybridization reaction

A novel label-free cascade amplification strategy based on dumbbell probe-mediated rolling circle amplification-responsive G-quadruplex formation for highly sensitive and selective detection of NAD(+) or ATP
A carbon nanotubes based ATP apta-sensing platform and its application in cellular assay

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ruyuan Zhang received her bachelor degree in school of food science and technology from Jiangnan university, and now is a master student in Nutrition and food hygiene at Jiangnan University. Her current research interest is the biosensors based on nanomaterials.

Jiadi Sun received her bachelor degree in school of environmental and chemical engineering from Tianjin polytechnic university, and now is a PhD student in Food science and Engineering at Jiangnan University. Her current research interests include mental nanomaterials and optical sensors.

Jian Ji received his PhD from Jiangnan University in 2017, and now is working at Jiangnan University as an associate professor in Food Safety and Quality Control. His current research interests include Electrochemical biosensors and inspection of food toxin.

Fuwei Pi received his PhD from Kwansei Gakuin University in 2011, and now is working at Jiangnan University as a professor in Food Safety and Quality Control. His current research interests include Spectroscopy and Spectral Analysis.

Yan Xiao received her M.A. in analytical chemistry from Guangxi Normal University in 2008, and now is a food testing and analysis laboratory director of GRG Metrology & test Nanning Co., Ltd. Her current research interest is the nanomaterials.
Yinzhi Zhang got his M.A. from Northwest A&F University, and now is working at Jiangnan University. His current research interests include Spectroscopy and Spectral Analysis.

Xiulan Sun received her PhD from Jiangnan University in 2005, and now is working at Jiangnan University as a professor in Food Safety and Quality Control. Her current research interests include Enzyme Biosensors and Cell Biosensors.
